# Chapter 15

[6d4426uj] The Charger and the Chevelle sat under tarps that breathed with the wind. The compound had gone quiet in the days after Phoenix, like the desert itself was holding its tongue. Even the solar panels seemed to glare less, filmed over with dust. Flynn walked the yard in the afternoon heat and tried to find a place inside himself that still felt clean. He kept coming up empty.

[z3yrbo8d] The news came in bursts on the cheap throwaway screens Birdie scavenged from the trash bins in town. Khoury’s face, bruised and swollen, then the house camera footage with the masked shapes and the blurred plates. A word like MANHUNT across the bottom, bold as a verdict. Birdie watched it like it was a highlight reel. Cage didn’t watch at all.

[4n0yl4dh] Cage sat in the shade of a shipping container with a wrench in his hand he wasn’t using, just turning it slowly, eyes half-lidded like a cat. He listened for engines on the horizon and for the little sounds people made when they were afraid. When Diesel passed him with a crate of parts he said, casual, “We’ll move the cars tomorrow.” She didn’t answer. Not even a look.

[7tj0fbjn] Diesel’s shop smelled like hot metal and old solvent and the faint sweet bite of fuel. She kept the door half-closed now. Flynn saw her through the gap, moving with that hard economy of motion, hair bound up, grease along her cheek like war paint. She worked the way some people prayed, head down, hands steady. When she turned and noticed him watching she didn’t tell him to come in. She didn’t tell him to leave.

[h7comail] Wrench had stopped singing under his breath. That was how Flynn noticed something had broken that wouldn’t weld back together. The older man sat on an upside-down bucket at night with his hands on his knees, looking out into the dark where the boneyard lights used to be, as if he was waiting for something to appear and absolve him. When Birdie cracked jokes Wrench smiled late, like it took effort.

[j1vhx77e] Birdie was the opposite. He moved through the compound like a man who’d just won a prizefight. He’d slap the tarps, call the cars beautiful, talk about Mexico and the smell of chillies and the sound of engines screaming across open country. He’d say, “We did it, man. We really did it.” Flynn nodded because it was easier than arguing with the gravity of Birdie’s joy.

[lem16mbc] The black market contact came at dusk in a pod that drove itself, quiet as an insect, and stopped outside the perimeter like it was afraid to cross the line. The driver got out anyway, a thin man in mirrored lenses, his hands clean, his boots too new. Cage walked out to meet him with his shoulders loose and his smile turned up. Flynn stayed back in the shadow of a container and watched the distance between men become a transaction.

[cnj66l2m] The contact kept his voice low. He said names like prayers: Sonora, Nogales, Castellanos. He said there were eyes on the roads now. He said Khoury had friends in government, in security, in the companies that made the pods run. Cage nodded like he was hearing weather. “We’re not going through the roads they watch,” Cage said. “We’re going through the roads they forgot.”

[vcw2az8a] Diesel came out only when Cage called for her. She stood apart, arms folded, listening. When the contact looked at her he looked away quickly. Cage offered prices that were almost insults, and the contact pretended to be offended, and Flynn could see the dance, the way men circled each other with words instead of knives. Diesel didn’t dance. She stared at Cage like she was measuring how much of him was myth and how much was rot.

[c7o6c3n5] The deal was simple: two cars would go. One would stay. Cash would come back in heat-sealed bricks and a fuel voucher in a code nobody would say out loud. Cage wanted the Charger to keep because it looked like violence even parked. Diesel wanted the Chevelle because it was honest power, steel and torque. Birdie wanted all of them, greedy like a child. Flynn wanted none.

[0tpwnrl6] When the contact left the compound in his pod, the air felt lighter for a moment, then heavier, because there was no one left to pretend to be civilized for. Cage clapped Flynn on the shoulder. “See?” he said. “It’s just work.” Flynn felt the touch through his shirt like a brand. He said, “Yeah.” The word came out flat.

[k3knb8gh] That night Cage cooked on the old grill with the broken lid. He used canned beans and a packet of dried meat and he made it taste like ash and triumph. Birdie ate fast, laughing, smearing sauce on his chin. Cage told a story about an old race outside Tucson, a time he’d rolled a car at a hundred and walked away. He described the moment the world turned upside down like it was a lover’s embrace.

[q0kvtq18] Diesel didn’t eat with them. Flynn took a bowl to her shop and knocked once. No answer. He set it by the door anyway. The metal door was warm from the day and it hummed faintly with the generator’s pulse. He stood there with his hand on the knob and he could hear her inside, moving, a tool tapping, a drawer closing. She wasn’t hiding from him. She was hiding from what he represented.

[f5qyc91a] The next morning a drone passed high overhead, a black speck against the washed-out sky. Flynn watched it until it vanished. Cage didn’t look up. He was under the Shelby’s hood, hands buried in guts, whistling something old. Birdie asked if the drone was looking for them. Cage said, “Everything is looking. It don’t mean it’s seeing.” Birdie laughed like that was wisdom.

[q1b29s77] Flynn kept thinking about Khoury’s eyes in the safe house, the way they had gone from outrage to pleading to a kind of quiet horror that didn’t have words. He remembered the children’s faces, pale in the dark hallway, their mouths open, their bodies frozen like prey. In the city the implants would smooth the shock, dull it, sell them something to make it feel better. Here the shock had nowhere to go.

[s7e0wa0h] The compound had its own rules. No calls out after dark. No driving in and out unless you had to. No lights from the road. The old culture had always been paranoid, but now it had a reason. Wrench kept checking the perimeter line, making sure the scrap fence was still wired. He did it with the patience of a man who knew fences were mostly faith.

[9wkl3vzm] Cage started calling Diesel by her real name again. Flynn had only heard it once before, in a story Birdie told about Cage pulling Diesel out of a wreck years back. Senna. It sounded too soft for her. Cage said it like he owned the syllables. “Senna,” he’d call, and she’d keep working as if she hadn’t heard, jaw clenched. The name in his mouth made Flynn uneasy.

[55kiu8lz] The confrontation happened in the late afternoon when the heat had begun to retreat but the air still held it, trapped in the tin walls. Flynn was carrying a box of spark plugs past Diesel’s shop when he heard her voice, low and sharp, and Cage’s answering laugh. The shop door was cracked open. He stopped without thinking, box heavy in his arms, and listened.

[uzcges5r] “This isn’t about the cars,” Diesel said. “You know it isn’t.” Her voice had no tremor. Flynn pictured her standing with her hands on her hips, eyes level. Cage’s voice came smooth. “Everything’s about the cars,” he said. “They’re the only honest thing left.” Diesel said, “Don’t do that. Don’t dress it up.”

[caz19fuc] Cage replied like he was indulging a child. “You’re mad because I took what we needed.” Diesel said, “I’m mad because you liked it.” Silence, then the sound of a tool hitting the bench. Cage’s laugh came again, quieter. “You think you know what I like.” Diesel said, “I was there. I watched you.”

[euhvyndk] Cage’s tone changed, losing some of the play. “We’re going to Mexico,” he said. “We’re going to run La Carrera. We’re going to win. This is the road.” Diesel said, “I understood racing. I understood risk. Not this.” Cage said, “This is risk.” Diesel answered, “No. This is power.”

[hk99aigz] Flynn leaned closer without meaning to, his shoulder brushing the corrugated metal. Cage said, “You used to understand.” Diesel said, “Don’t talk like you miss me.” Cage said, “I miss the part of you that didn’t flinch.” Diesel said, “I didn’t flinch because I didn’t know who you were.”

[f119e545] Cage’s voice went cold, clean as a blade. “Then leave.” Diesel said nothing for a long moment. Flynn could hear her breathing, slow, controlled. Cage spoke again, softer, dangerous in its gentleness. “You won’t,” he said. “You love this too much. You love the work. You love the feeling of making something illegal breathe.”

[5fqg99e9] Diesel finally said, “I love the engines. Not you.” Cage made a sound like he was amused and hurt at once, a performance Flynn could almost believe if he hadn’t seen Cage’s eyes when Khoury begged. Cage said, “You’re still here.” Diesel said, “So are you.” Cage said, “I’m exactly where I belong.”

[9z6t10ak] The door shifted. Flynn stepped back fast, heart kicking, and the box in his arms rattled. Diesel came out first. She saw him. The look she gave him wasn’t anger. It was something worse, like disappointment that had hardened into a tool. “You need those plugs?” she asked. Her voice was normal now. Flynn said, “Yeah.” He held the box out like an offering.

[jqeihe6n] Cage followed her into the light. He smiled at Flynn as if they were sharing a joke. “Eavesdropping?” he asked. Flynn felt his face heat. He said, “I was just—” Cage cut him off with a shrug. “Doesn’t matter. We’re family. Family hears everything.” Diesel walked past them, wiping her hands on a rag that was already black.

[m8tz2pe5] Later, as the sun went down, Cage took Birdie out to the cars and talked about routes and checkpoints and old washes where you could run without lights. Flynn watched from the porch of the main container, a beer sweating in his hand. Birdie leaned in close like a student. Cage drew lines in the dust with a stick, making the desert into a map and a promise.

[u9vky0w9] Wrench found Flynn sitting there and lowered himself beside him with a groan. The older man smelled of cigarettes and sunburn. He didn’t ask for the beer. He stared at the yard where Birdie and Cage stood like silhouettes against the last light. “He’s got the kid,” Wrench said quietly. Flynn said, “He’s got all of us.” Wrench nodded once.

[a4wsa62z] Flynn asked, “How long have you known him?” Wrench laughed without humor. “Since before the ban. Since we were stealing fuel out of construction sites and thinking we were kings.” He spat into the dirt. “This isn’t new. It’s just bigger now. Back then there were limits. We had to worry about cops, about dying. Now the world tells him he can’t die. So he thinks he can’t be wrong.”

[5smjllhu] Flynn stared at his hands. They were cracked now, grime in the lines, the hands of someone who could take an engine apart and put it back. He’d wanted that. He’d wanted to be useful. “What do we do?” he asked. The question came out small. Wrench said, “We stop pretending. We do what we should’ve done the first time he smiled and we followed.”

[fhc2qkzu] Flynn said, “Leave?” Wrench’s eyes stayed on Cage. “After we sell the cars,” he said. “After the money’s in my hand. I’m taking my cut and I’m gone. I’ll disappear into some place where pods don’t come and drones don’t bother. Maybe I’ll go north where it still rains sometimes.” Flynn felt a twist of panic. “He’ll let you?”

[alvjlgbd] Wrench shrugged. “He’ll say he will. That’s the trick.” The desert went purple at the edges and the first stars came out like pinholes in a tarp. Wrench’s face looked older in the fading light, carved by regret. “He doesn’t do revenge like other men,” Wrench said. “He does it like a lesson. Like he’s teaching you what the world is.”

[wl09js0h] Flynn swallowed. “What about Birdie?” he asked. Wrench sighed. “Bring him if you can,” he said. “But he won’t come. He worships Cage. Cage gave him a story to live in. Birdie would rather die in that story than walk out into the plain daylight of being nobody.” Flynn watched Birdie laugh at something Cage said, head thrown back, mouth open, unguarded.

[gjjdceoh] Flynn said, “Diesel might leave.” Wrench looked at him then, really looked, as if seeing how young Flynn still was under the dirt. “She should,” Wrench said. “But she’s like me. She thinks she can keep the fire in the stove. She thinks she can use him without getting burned.” He nodded toward the shop. “She knows better. That’s why she’s angry.”

[eg29yg45] The cash came the next day in the back of the same self-driving pod, packed in a cooler like meat. The contact didn’t linger. He didn’t even step fully inside the perimeter. Cage carried the cooler like it was nothing and set it on the table in the main container. Birdie whistled. Flynn looked at the bricks of money and felt sick. It looked too clean for what it had cost.

[9dfnmike] Cage broke the bricks open and counted them with quick fingers, faster than a machine. He spoke as he counted, talking about fuel and tires and how many spare filters you needed for desert dust. He talked about Mexico like it was a promised land. Diesel stood in the doorway, arms crossed, watching the cash like it might bite. Wrench kept his hands in his pockets.

[rfkwfo3x] When Cage handed Wrench a share, the older man hesitated. Cage watched him with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Take it,” Cage said. “You earned it.” Wrench took the money like he was taking a loaded gun. Birdie looked between them, confused, sensing something but not understanding. Flynn wanted to say something, anything, but his throat had closed.

[wkyu69eq] After the count, Cage stepped outside and lit a cigarette he didn’t inhale, just held between his lips for show. Flynn followed him out because he didn’t know what else to do. The desert wind tugged at Cage’s hair. “You heard us yesterday,” Cage said. It wasn’t a question. Flynn said, “I didn’t mean to.” Cage shrugged. “Intent’s for church.”

[x7b6fc1j] Cage looked out past the scrap fence toward the empty land. “Senna thinks she’s better than this,” he said softly. “She thinks she’s the last good person in a bad room.” He turned his eyes to Flynn. “You ever notice the good ones stay the longest? Because they think they’re needed.” Flynn felt the words sink in like hooks. Cage smiled. “You’re needed too.”

[6f5nksi6] Flynn said, “Khoury had kids.” The sentence sounded childish, like something a teacher would scold. Cage didn’t flinch. “And?” he said. “Kids grow. Kids forget. Their implants will show them ads for a better feeling.” He flicked ash into the dirt. “You’re acting like we did something permanent.” Flynn said, “We did.” Cage’s smile thinned.

[8yds4r39] “Permanent’s a lie,” Cage said. “This whole world is built on undo. Undo the heat with air-conditioning. Undo hunger with synth. Undo death with a plug-in.” He tapped his chest where his own implant sat under the skin. “The only thing they can’t undo is the fact that you were scared and then you weren’t. That you did it.” He leaned closer. “That’s what’s real.”

[780pufyh] Flynn wanted to argue but his mind kept showing him Khoury on the floor, wrists bound, the small wet sound he made when Cage hit him. Flynn heard himself say, “It felt wrong.” Cage nodded like that pleased him. “Good,” he said. “If it felt right you’d be dead inside. Wrong means you’re awake. Hold onto that. It’s the last honest thing you’ve got.”

[i8yapbug] That night Flynn lay on his bunk and listened to the compound settle. Somewhere out in the dark Diesel’s shop light flicked on, then off, then on again. Cage’s laughter carried from the yard where Birdie still hovered close. Wrench’s footsteps crossed the gravel slow, like he was measuring distance. Flynn stared at the ceiling and tried to imagine leaving. The image wouldn’t hold.

[ore5e87r] Near dawn, when the air was briefly cool and the sky had that pale bruised look, Flynn went outside. The tarps over the cars moved with the wind like bodies breathing. He thought about Mexico, about the race, about the way Cage talked about it with a hunger that didn’t stop at winning. He thought about Diesel’s silence and Wrench’s tired eyes and Birdie’s bright, doomed faith.

[n0b5k5gx] He stood there until the sun’s first edge came up and turned the scrap fence into a line of knives. In that new light the compound looked less like a home and more like a camp, temporary by nature, something meant to pack up fast and vanish. Flynn realized the choice wasn’t waiting for him like a fork in the road. It was already under his feet, a slow crumble. He felt it give.

[71bzgjfe] When Birdie came out yawning and saw Flynn, he grinned. “We’re really doing it,” he said. “Mexico. La Carrera.” Flynn looked at him and saw a kid who wanted a story and didn’t know stories ate people. Flynn nodded. “Yeah,” he said, because he didn’t know how to say goodbye. Somewhere behind them Cage’s engine turned over, loud and hungry, and the day began like a threat.
